---
title: "1.2 Common Pitfalls of Backtesting"
subtitle: "Video lesson slides"
author: "Algorithmic Trading"
notes: |
  Backtesting errors are not usually neutral. Many of them make the historical
  market easier than the live market: the signal knows too much, the stock
  universe is cleaner, the price is more convenient, or the venue is not the
  one we can trade. This lesson turns the pitfall list into one diagnostic
  question: did the backtest give the strategy an advantage it would not have
  had in real time?

format:
  revealjs:
    theme: default
    css: ../../../../styles/reveal.css
    slide-number: true
    controls: true
    progress: true
    menu: false
    transition: slide
    transition-speed: fast
    center: false
    auto-stretch: true
    auto-animate: true
    show-notes: false
    scrollable: true
    embed-resources: false
filters:
  - ../../../../scripts/lua/remove-reveal-notes.lua
include-before-body:
  - ../../../../styles/reveal-audience-only.html
execute:
  echo: true
---

## Backtesting pitfalls usually make the past easier to trade than the future.

| Shortcut in the test | Constraint in live trading      | Pitfall family       |
| -------------------- | ------------------------------- | -------------------- |
| future fields        | current information only         | look-ahead           |
| tuned holdout        | unseen data after rule freeze    | data-snooping        |
| surviving stocks     | delistings and borrow limits     | universe and access  |
| convenient prices    | executable auctions and quotes   | price and venue      |

::: {.notes}
Frame the section as one repeated pattern. The backtest gives the strategy
something that live trading would not give it: future information, a cleaned-up
universe, a price it could not fill, or a venue it could not access. The result
is usually not harmless noise. It is performance that is too good.
:::

## Look-ahead bias lets the signal know the ending before the trade begins.

::: {.visual-slide}
::: {.visual-frame}
![](figures/look-ahead-timing.svg){fig-alt="Flow showing that same-day high and low are unknown at the morning decision, so the entry signal cannot use them"}
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Look-ahead bias is the clearest example. If a morning entry signal uses the
same day's high or low, the backtest has given the strategy knowledge from the
end of the day. A live system cannot have that value at the decision time, so
the backtest is no longer testing a real-time prediction rule.
:::

## The signal must be observable before the return is credited.

```python
# unsafe when high_t is not known yet
raw_signal_t = high_t > threshold

# safer shape: trade one period after the signal is known
signal_t = raw_signal.shift(1)
```

::: {.notes}
The timeline test is simple: the signal must exist before the trade earns the
return. Shifting the signal is not a mechanical afterthought. It enforces the
order of events. First information becomes available, then the strategy decides,
then the next return belongs to that decision.
:::

## Shared strategy logic removes one hiding place for future information.

::: {.visual-slide}
::: {.visual-frame}
![](figures/shared-strategy-function.svg){fig-alt="Historical feed and live feed both entering one strategy function that produces orders"}
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Chan's preferred defense is structural. If the backtest and the live system use
the same strategy function, and only the data feed changes, future information
has fewer places to sneak in. This does not prove the strategy is good, but it
helps prevent a backtest-only programming error.
:::

## A holdout set stops being clean after we tune the model to pass it.

::: {.visual-slide}
::: {.visual-frame}
![](figures/holdout-tuning-loop.svg){fig-alt="Loop showing holdout testing, poor result, parameter tweaking, and retesting on the same holdout"}
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Data-snooping bias is more subtle because it can happen during honest research.
We test a model on a holdout set, dislike the result, tweak the model, and test
again. After enough loops, the holdout set has become part of training. It is no
longer a clean estimate of future performance.
:::

## Simple linear rules reduce the number of ways to fit noise.

| Design choice        | Freedom to fit history           | Diagnostic question          |
| -------------------- | -------------------------------- | ---------------------------- |
| many nonlinear rules | many thresholds and exceptions   | Did complexity predict?      |
| few linear terms     | fewer knobs to tune              | Can a simpler rule work?     |
| equal-weight ranks   | less dependence on sample quirks | Are weights doing real work? |

::: {.notes}
The defense is not simplicity for its own sake. The defense is that every extra
rule, threshold, distributional assumption, or parameter is another opportunity
to fit historical accidents. A more complex model should earn its complexity by
predicting better, not merely by explaining the past more tightly.
:::

## Walk-forward testing is the final defense because every research loop leaks.

| Test stage      | What it can reveal                         |
| --------------- | ------------------------------------------ |
| cross-validation | whether results survive different subsets |
| paper trading   | whether the frozen rule works after research |
| small live trade | whether real execution frictions appear   |

::: {.notes}
Cross-validation helps when data are scarce, especially if performance is
stable across subsets. But Chan is blunt about the final defense: because some
data-snooping almost always creeps in, the frozen model needs walk-forward
testing. Paper trading helps, and a small real-money deployment can reveal
execution issues that even paper trading misses.
:::

## Splits and dividends can turn accounting events into false signals.

| Event                | Quoted price | Holdings | Economic value |
| -------------------- | -----------: | -------: | -------------: |
| Before 2-for-1 split |          100 |        1 |            100 |
| After 2-for-1 split  |           50 |        2 |            100 |
| If unadjusted        |         -50% |          | false signal   |

::: {.notes}
Now move from model selection to data preparation. A split changes the quoted
price and the share count, not the economic value of the position. Dividends
create a similar mechanical price drop. If the historical series is not
adjusted before the signal sees it, accounting mechanics become fake alpha.
:::

## Survivor-only data makes recovery look obvious after it has happened.

::: {.visual-slide}
::: {.visual-frame}
![](figures/survivorship-database.svg){fig-alt="Database filter showing survived stocks included and delisted or failed stocks missing"}
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Survivorship bias changes the universe. If the database contains only stocks
that still exist today, the backtest acts as if the trader knew which distressed
companies would survive. That is especially dangerous for long-only
mean-reversion rules that buy the biggest recent losers.
:::

## The bias is strongest for long-only mean reversion and weaker for momentum.

| Strategy type             | Missing delisted names usually do what? |
| ------------------------- | --------------------------------------- |
| long-only mean reversion  | inflate returns                         |
| short-only mean reversion | deflate returns                         |
| long-short mean reversion | still biased, often net inflated        |
| momentum                  | often deflate returns                   |

::: {.notes}
Chan is careful here about direction. If bankrupt stocks disappear from the
 database, long-only mean reversion looks too good because the worst losers that
 should have gone to zero are silently removed. Short-only mean reversion can
 look worse for the opposite reason, and momentum often loses some profitable
 short trades when failed stocks are missing.
:::

## Short-sale constraints can erase trades that look easy in a database.

| Backtest assumption       | Live-market question                     |
| ------------------------- | ---------------------------------------- |
| any stock can be shorted  | Could the broker locate shares?          |
| borrow is always cheap    | Was the name hard to borrow?             |
| market order can execute  | Did short-sale rules block the fill?     |

::: {.notes}
Short-sale constraints are another universe problem, but now the issue is
access rather than membership. A backtest may assume every short trade can be
placed. Live trading depends on locate availability, borrow cost, share float,
short interest, and occasionally rule-based restrictions. Ignoring these
constraints can create profits that no trader could have captured.
:::

## Uptick rules can block the fill even when the signal is correct.

| Constraint source         | What it changes in practice                |
| ------------------------- | ------------------------------------------ |
| hard-to-borrow status     | borrow may be expensive or unavailable     |
| low float / high short interest | desired size may not be borrowable   |
| SEC short-sale restrictions | short market order may not fill         |

::: {.notes}
The book gives a concrete warning: regulation can change whether a short order
 is executable at all. Under the original uptick rule, and later under the
 alternative uptick rule after a circuit breaker is triggered, a short market
 order may be prevented from filling. A realistic short backtest has to model
 these constraints, not just direction.
:::

## Auction orders need primary-exchange auction prices.

| Live order type      | Historical price needed                  |
| -------------------- | ---------------------------------------- |
| Market-on-open       | primary opening auction                  |
| Market-on-close      | primary closing auction                  |
| Signal from high/low | representative executable price range    |

::: {.notes}
Next, match the price to the order. U.S. stocks trade across many venues, but
market-on-open and market-on-close orders route to the primary exchange
auction. Consolidated daily open and close prices may come from another venue,
so they can be the wrong evidence for the order we are testing.
:::

## Consolidated outliers can create fills the auction never offered.

::: {.visual-slide}
::: {.visual-frame}
![](figures/auction-price-mismatch.svg){fig-alt="Comparison showing secondary venue odd print is not equal to primary auction fill"}
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
The mistake is especially tempting in mean-reversion tests. A small trade on a
secondary venue can print away from the primary auction price. The next price
may revert, and the backtest records a profit. But the strategy may never have
been able to fill at that outlier price in meaningful size.
:::

## Forex backtests are realistic only on executable venue quotes.

| Data source           | Tradable by the strategy? | What must be checked      |
| --------------------- | ------------------------- | ------------------------- |
| single bank or ECN    | maybe                     | venue-specific spread     |
| aggregated order book | only if executable        | depth and access          |
| delayed trade prints  | usually not enough        | bid-ask quotes preferred  |

::: {.notes}
Foreign exchange makes the venue problem even sharper. There is no single
central exchange and no rule that every venue must execute at the best bid or
ask across the market. A forex backtest is realistic only if its quotes come
from the venue set the live strategy can actually trade, with that venue set's
spreads and transaction costs.
:::

## A clean backtest passes five market-reality gates before performance matters.

| Gate        | Passing evidence                                  |
| ----------- | ------------------------------------------------- |
| Information | no future fields in current signals               |
| Selection   | validation data are not reused for tuning         |
| Universe    | delisted and hard-to-borrow names are represented |
| Price       | corporate actions and auction prices are handled  |
| Venue       | quotes and spreads match executable venues        |

::: {.notes}
End with the diagnostic checklist. Before the Sharpe ratio matters, the
backtest has to pass five gates: information, selection, universe, price, and
venue. If one gate fails, the result may still be interesting, but it is
evidence about a friendlier market than the one the strategy would face live.
:::
